en.extract:
The pale-headed brushfinch (Atlapetes pallidiceps) is a species of bird in the family Passerellidae. It is endemic to the Jubones River drainage in south-central Ecuador. 


Description
Atlapetes pallidiceps was considered to be possibly extinct before its rediscovery in 1998, having last been recorded in 1969.
The species eats insects, seeds, and fruit, and engages in perch-gleaning behavior.


Vocalizations
Male pale-headed brushfinches are the only sex that appears to vocalize in this species; their song is composed of 5 - 12 notes separated into three groups. These songs appear to be used primarily for the communication of territorial bounds, more commonly in young birds. A. pallidiceps songs are comparable to the songs of other Atlapetes members.


Habitat
Atlapetes pallidiceps inhabits a less than 4 km² area of the Yunguilla Valley, characterized by sparsely-vegetated landslide rifts and fallow fields at elevations between 1,650–1,900 meters. Males have an average territory size of 0.7–1.4 hectares, typically within 100 meters of a stream, which generally contains large stands of Chusquea sp. bamboo.


Conservation
The pale-headed brushfinch is classified as endangered, with total population of the species estimated to be 240–340 individuals (160–226 mature individuals). 
This species has historically been heavily threatened by brood parasitism from the shiny cowbird (Molothrus bonariensis), with an estimated 38.5% of all A. pallidiceps nests being parasitized. After a management plan which included mass culling of cowbirds, the population has increased by a magnitude of 5 times pre-intervention size. 
Upon the species' rediscovery, the population was very small, having only 12-22 breeding males across the Yunguilla Reserve in 1999. Due to aggressive conservation management, the population rapidly increased to a peak size of 110-120 distinct, male-occupied territories in 2008. These conservation efforts are believed to have stabilized the population, but the species remains restricted to the small Yunguilla Reserve and may have reached the carrying capacity of its current habitat.
The pale-headed brush-finch may compete for resources or be displaced from areas outside its small, known habitat by the white-browned brushfinch (Burraremon torquatus), rufous-collared sparrow (Zonotrichia capensis), and congeners Bolivian brushfinch (A. rufincha) and white-winged brushfinch (A. leucopterus).


References


External links
BirdLife International species factsheet

The '''pale-headed brushfinch''' ('''''Atlapetes pallidiceps''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Passerellidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the [[Jubones River]] drainage in south-central [[Ecuador]]. 

== Description ==
''Atlapetes pallidiceps'' was considered to be possibly extinct before its rediscovery in 1998, having last been recorded in 1969.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last=Agreda |first=Ana |last2=Krabbe |first2=Niels |last3=Rodriguez |first3=Orfa |date=1999 |title=Pale-headed Brush-finch ''Atlapetes pallidiceps'' is not extinct |journal=Cotinga |volume=11 |pages=50-54}}</ref>

The species eats insects, seeds, and fruit, and engages in perch-gleaning behavior.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=Krabbe |first=Niels |date=2004 |title=Pale-headed Brush-finch ''Atlapetes pallidiceps'': notes on population size, habitat, vocalizations, feeding, interference competition and conservation |url=https://doi.org/10.1017/S0959270904000115 |journal=Bird Conservation International |volume=14 |issue=2 |pages=77-86}}</ref>

== Vocalizations ==
Male pale-headed brushfinches are the only sex that appears to vocalize in this species; their song is composed of 5 - 12 notes separated into three groups. These songs appear to be used primarily for the communication of territorial bounds, more commonly in young birds. ''A. pallidiceps'' songs are comparable to the songs of other ''Atlapetes'' members.<ref name=":1" />

== Habitat ==
''Atlapetes pallidiceps'' inhabits a less than 4 km² area of the Yunguilla Valley, characterized by sparsely-vegetated landslide rifts and fallow fields at elevations between 1,650–1,900 meters.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021">{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2020 |title=''Atlapetes pallidiceps'' |volume=2020 |article-number=e.T22721487A181562033 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2020-3.RLTS.T22721487A181562033.en |access-date=12 November 2021}}</ref><ref name=":1" /> Males have an average territory size of 0.7–1.4 hectares, typically within 100 meters of a stream, which generally contains large stands of ''[[Chusquea]]'' sp. bamboo.<ref name=":1" />

== Conservation ==
The pale-headed brushfinch is classified as [[Endangered species|endangered]], with total population of the species estimated to be 240–340 individuals (160–226 mature individuals).<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> 

This species has historically been heavily threatened by [[brood parasitism]] from the [[shiny cowbird]] (''Molothrus bonariensis''), with an estimated 38.5% of all ''A. pallidiceps'' nests being parasitized.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Oppel |first=Steffen |last2=Schaeffer |first2=H. Martin |last3=Schmidt |first3=Veronika |last4=Schröder |first4=Boris |date=2004 |title=Cowbird parasitism of Pale-headed Brush-finch ''Atlapetes pallidiceps'': implications for conservation and management |url=https://doi.org/10.1017/S0959270904000103 |journal=Bird Conservation International |volume=14 |issue=2 |pages=63-75}}</ref> After a management plan which included mass culling of cowbirds, the population has increased by a magnitude of 5 times pre-intervention size.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Krabbe |first=Niels |last2=Juiña |first2=Mery |last3=Fernando Sornoza |first3=Aldo |date=2011 |title=Marked population increase in Pale-headed Brush-finch ''Atlapetes pallidiceps'' in response to cowbird control |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s10336-010-0567-z |journal=Journal of Ornithology |volume=152 |pages=219-222}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last=Hartmann |first=Stefanie A. |last2=Oppel |first2=Steffen |last3=Segelbacher |first3=Gernot |last4=Juiña |first4=Mery E. |last5=Schaefer |first5=H. Martin |date=2017 |title=Decline in territory size and fecundity as a response to carrying capacity in an endangered songbird |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s00442-016-3763-6 |journal=Oecologia |volume=183 |pages=597-606}}</ref> 

Upon the species' rediscovery, the population was very small, having only 12-22 breeding males across the Yunguilla Reserve in 1999. Due to aggressive conservation management, the population rapidly increased to a peak size of 110-120 distinct, male-occupied territories in 2008.<ref>{{Cite web |title=BirdLife DataZone |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/pale-headed-brushfinch-atlapetes-pallidiceps |access-date=2026-03-10 |website=datazone.birdlife.org |language=en}}</ref> These conservation efforts are believed to have stabilized the population, but the species remains restricted to the small Yunguilla Reserve and may have reached the [[carrying capacity]] of its current habitat.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /><ref name=":2" />

The pale-headed brush-finch may compete for resources or be displaced from areas outside its small, known habitat by the [[White-browed brushfinch|white-browned brushfinch]] (''Burraremon torquatus''), [[rufous-collared sparrow]] (''Zonotrichia capensis''), and congeners [[Bolivian brushfinch]] (''A. rufincha'') and [[white-winged brushfinch]] (''A. leucopterus'').<ref name=":0" />
